story:
Reginald, a young beaver, admires an oak tree’s orange leaves, bites the bark, and tries to work because he needs a lodge before winter. Since a red-coated fur trapper drowned his parents and his brother Bertram, then carried them off, he is alone and frightened. The oak on the grassy bank is mighty, as Bertram once said, and memories dull his bite. Tears come; he fights the sickness that rises when he recalls the sack and the trapper’s boots. His jaw aches; he must search downstream for a smaller tree near a good site.

He trudges past fields dusted with pesticides until an apple tree appears, slender and growing beside a narrow bend where water runs deep enough for a lodge. “Oh my! Oh my!” He works until his jaw, neck, and back burn, christens his future home Little Warblerbranch—or perhaps Bertram’s Castle—and sleeps. At dawn he chews fast, and the trunk shivers so hard that a nest hidden in the lower branches jerks sideways—then a flash of black with purples and greens spears his side. A starling, lord of the apple, shrieks that Reginald has ruined his safe home. Reginald sobs, explains his loss, and begs forgiveness. The starling, frightened and furious, calls him a thief and drives him off.

Night drops quickly. Reginald prays behind a shrub for safety—and for Starling. The bird’s screams rip the dark: an owl is on him. Reginald can’t bear it. He thunders out, slaps his broad tail so the crack startles the owl, and shields Starling by a patch of dandelions. The owl declares the scene undignified and decides a struggling bird is no longer worth such noisy trouble, then sails away. Shaken, the two share the shrub, promising to keep watch, and fall asleep because companionship quiets the heart.

In the pale days that follow they decide to seek a faraway nature sanctuary because frost is hardening the banks, Reginald cannot finish a lodge in time, and Starling says he has heard migrating birds speak of a refuge where poisoned fields and trappers are kept out. Starling skims above and talks of his own family, poisoned by humans’ carelessness; he apologizes for his insults—apple is fine beaver timber, and his anger was not fact. He also clears Reginald’s secret shame: crows who flew over the valley had seen human drones circling the beaver colony days before the trapper came; Reginald’s wandering did not doom it. Air returns to Reginald’s lungs. They agree that hope can walk beside grief. Starling repeats a peacock’s scolding that he is “too negative,” and Reginald counters that choosing friendship with the very beaver who had begun to fell his home, and setting out for a place that might not exist, is proof of hope.

Frost sews the meadows. Then a red flash: the trapper. A blow; blackness. He wakes in a canvas bag, gnaws the man’s hand, spills out onto the snow, under a club that thumps his tail, and runs. Starling, who had been following overhead, dives like a meteor, claws and pecks the trapper’s eyes and ear. Caught and hurt, he falls; Reginald bites the man’s ankle until he flees. Starling’s wing hangs wrong. With string scavenged from the stream, Reginald loops the strand through a forked stick and trailing bark, making a rough sling he can drag while Starling lies inside, and bears his friend onward.

Snow begins in earnest. A cabin appears, chimney smoking, a sign out front. Starling reads it: North Marsh Wildlife Clinic—Sanctuary Boundary. Reginald thumps the door; a kind woman brings them in.

Seasons turn. Starling’s wing heals; Reginald finds other beavers already living within the protected marshes of the sanctuary; together they admire dandelions, bluebells, and mushrooms. In that refuge, it is well known that the starling and the beaver, who met in anger and stood together in danger, remained the very best of friends for life.
